utterances.scene_summary: Romeo hides from his friends after the feast.

utterances.speaker: MERCUTIO
utterances.text: He is wise, And, on my life, hath stol'n him home to bed.
utterances.location: A lane by the wall of Capulet's orchard
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo hides from his friends after the feast.

utterances.speaker: BENVOLIO
utterances.text: He ran this way, and leapt this orchard wall. Call, good Mercutio.
utterances.location: A lane by the wall of Capulet's orchard
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo hides from his friends after the feast.

utterances.speaker: MERCUTIO
utterances.text: Nay, I'll conjure too. Romeo! humours! madman! passion! lover! Appear thou in the likeness of a sigh; Speak but one rhyme, and I am satisfied! Cry but 'Ah me!' pronounce but 'love' and 'dove'; Speak to my gossip Venus one fair word, One nickname for her purblind son and heir, Young auburn Cupid, he that shot so trim When King Cophetua lov'd the beggar maid! He heareth not, he stirreth not, be moveth not; The ape is dead, and I must conjure him. I conjure thee by Rosaline's bright eyes. By her high forehead and her scarlet lip, By her fine foot, straight leg, and quivering thigh, And the demesnes that there adjacent lie, That in thy likeness thou appear to us!
utterances.location: A lane by the wall of Capulet's orchard
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo hides from his friends after the feast.

utterances.speaker: BENVOLIO
utterances.text: An if he hear thee, thou wilt anger him.
utterances.location: A lane by the wall of Capulet's orchard
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo hides from his friends after the feast.

utterances.speaker: MERCUTIO
utterances.text: This cannot anger him. 'Twould anger him To raise a spirit in his mistress' circle Of some strange nature, letting it there stand Till she had laid it and conjur'd it down. That were some spite; my invocation Is fair and honest: in his mistress' name, I conjure only but to raise up him.
utterances.location: A lane by the wall of Capulet's orchard
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo hides from his friends after the feast.

utterances.speaker: BENVOLIO
utterances.text: Come, he hath hid himself among these trees To be consorted with the humorous night. Blind is his love and best befits the dark.
utterances.location: A lane by the wall of Capulet's orchard
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo hides from his friends after the feast.

utterances.speaker: MERCUTIO
utterances.text: If love be blind, love cannot hit the mark. Now will he sit under a medlar tree And wish his mistress were that kind of fruit As maids call medlars when they laugh alone. O, Romeo, that she were, O that she were An open et cetera, thou a pop'rin pear! Romeo, good night. I'll to my truckle-bed; This field-bed is too cold for me to sleep. Come, shall we go?
utterances.location: A lane by the wall of Capulet's orchard
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo hides from his friends after the feast.

utterances.speaker: BENVOLIO
utterances.text: Go then, for 'tis in vain 'To seek him here that means not to be found.
utterances.location: A lane by the wall of Capulet's orchard
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo hides from his friends after the feast.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: Exeunt.
utterances.location: A lane by the wall of Capulet's orchard
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo hides from his friends after the feast.

scenes.text:
[Enter Romeo alone.]
ROMEO. Can I go forward when my heart is here? Turn back, dull earth, and find thy centre out.
[Climbs the wall and leaps down within it.]
[Enter Benvolio with Mercutio.]
BENVOLIO. Romeo! my cousin Romeo! Romeo!
MERCUTIO. He is wise, And, on my life, hath stol'n him home to bed.
BENVOLIO. He ran this way, and leapt this orchard wall. Call, good Mercutio.
MERCUTIO. Nay, I'll conjure too. Romeo! humours! madman! passion! lover! Appear thou in the likeness of a sigh; Speak but one rhyme, and I am satisfied! Cry but 'Ah me!' pronounce but 'love' and 'dove'; Speak to my gossip Venus one fair word, One nickname for her purblind son and heir, Young auburn Cupid, he that shot so trim When King Cophetua lov'd the beggar maid! He heareth not, he stirreth not, be moveth not; The ape is dead, and I must conjure him. I conjure thee by Rosaline's bright eyes. By her high forehead and her scarlet lip, By her fine foot, straight leg, and quivering thigh, And the demesnes that there adjacent lie, That in thy likeness thou appear to us!
BENVOLIO. An if he hear thee, thou wilt anger him.
MERCUTIO. This cannot anger him. 'Twould anger him To raise a spirit in his mistress' circle Of some strange nature, letting it there stand Till she had laid it and conjur'd it down. That were some spite; my invocation Is fair and honest: in his mistress' name, I conjure only but to raise up him.
BENVOLIO. Come, he hath hid himself among these trees To be consorted with the humorous night. Blind is his love and best befits the dark.
MERCUTIO. If love be blind, love cannot hit the mark. Now will he sit under a medlar tree And wish his mistress were that kind of fruit As maids call medlars when they laugh alone. O, Romeo, that she were, O that she were An open et cetera, thou a pop'rin pear! Romeo, good night. I'll to my truckle-bed; This field-bed is too cold for me to sleep. Come, shall we go?
BENVOLIO. Go then, for 'tis in vain 'To seek him here that means not to be found.
[Exeunt.]
scenes.scene_summary: Romeo hides from his friends after the feast.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: Enter Romeo.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: He jests at scars that never felt a wound.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: Enter Juliet above at a window.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: But soft! What light through yonder window breaks?
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: It is the East, and Juliet is the sun!
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: Arise, fair sun, and kill the envious moon,
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: Who is already sick and pale with grief
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: That thou her maid art far more fair than she.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: Be not her maid, since she is envious.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: Her vestal livery is but sick and green,
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: And none but fools do wear it. Cast it off.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: It is my lady; O, it is my love!
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: O that she knew she were!
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: She speaks, yet she says nothing. What of that?
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: Her eye discourses; I will answer it.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: I am too bold; 'tis not to me she speaks.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: Two of the fairest stars in all the heaven,
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: Having some business, do entreat her eyes
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: To twinkle in their spheres till they return.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: What if her eyes were there, they in her head?
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: The brightness of her cheek would shame those stars
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: As daylight doth a lamp; her eyes in heaven
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: Would through the airy region stream so bright
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: That birds would sing and think it were not night.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: See how she leans her cheek upon her hand!
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: O that I were a glove upon that hand,
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: That I might touch that cheek!
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: JULIET
utterances.text: Ah me!
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: She speaks. O, speak again, bright angel! for thou art As glorious to this night, being o'er my head, As is a winged messenger of heaven Unto the white-upturned wond'ring eyes Of mortals that fall back to gaze on him When he bestrides the lazy-pacing clouds And sails upon the bosom of the air.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: JULIET
utterances.text: O Romeo, Romeo! wherefore art thou Romeo? Deny thy father and refuse thy name! Or, if thou wilt not, be but sworn my love, And I'll no longer be a Capulet.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: [aside] Shall I hear more, or shall I speak at this?
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: JULIET
utterances.text: 'Tis but thy name that is my enemy. Thou art thyself, though not a Montague. What's Montague? it is nor hand, nor foot, Nor arm, nor face, nor any other part Belonging to a man. O, be some other name! What's in a name? That which we call a rose By any other name would smell as sweet. So Romeo would, were he not Romeo call'd, Retain that dear perfection which he owes Without that title. Romeo, doff thy name; And for that name, which is no part of thee, Take all myself.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: I take thee at thy word. Call me but love, and I'll be new baptiz'd; Henceforth I never will be Romeo.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: JULIET
utterances.text: What man art thou that, thus bescreen'd in night, So stumblest on my counsel?
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: By a name I know not how to tell thee who I am. My name, dear saint, is hateful to myself, Because it is an enemy to thee. Had I it written, I would tear the word.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: JULIET
utterances.text: My ears have yet not drunk a hundred words Of that tongue's utterance, yet I know the sound. Art thou not Romeo, and a Montague?
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: Neither, fair saint, if either thee dislike.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: JULIET
utterances.text: How cam'st thou hither, tell me, and wherefore? The orchard walls are high and hard to climb, And the place death, considering who thou art, If any of my kinsmen find thee here.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: With love's light wings did I o'erperch these walls; For stony limits cannot hold love out, And what love can do, that dares love attempt. Therefore thy kinsmen are no let to me.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: JULIET
utterances.text: If they do see thee, they will murther thee.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: Alack, there lies more peril in thine eye Than twenty of their swords! Look thou but sweet, And I am proof against their enmity.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: JULIET
utterances.text: I would not for the world they saw thee here.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: I have night's cloak to hide me from their sight; And but thou love me, let them find me here. My life were better ended by their hate Than death prorogued, wanting of thy love.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: JULIET
utterances.text: By whose direction found'st thou out this place?
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: By love, that first did prompt me to enquire. He lent me counsel, and I lent him eyes. I am no pilot; yet, wert thou as far As that vast shore wash'd with the farthest sea, I would adventure for such merchandise.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: JULIET
utterances.text: Thou knowest the mask of night is on my face; Else would a maiden blush bepaint my cheek For that which thou hast heard me speak to-night. Fain would I dwell on form- fain, fain deny What I have spoke; but farewell compliment! Dost thou love me, I know thou wilt say 'Ay'; And I will take thy word. Yet, if thou swear'st, Thou mayst prove false. At lovers' perjuries, They say Jove laughs. O gentle Romeo, If thou dost love, pronounce it faithfully. Or if thou thinkest I am too quickly won, I'll frown, and be perverse, and say thee nay, So thou wilt woo; but else, not for the world. In truth, fair Montague, I am too fond, And therefore thou mayst think my haviour light; But trust me, gentleman, I'll prove more true Than those that have more cunning to be strange. I should have been more strange, I must confess, But that thou overheard'st, ere I was ware, My true-love passion. Therefore pardon me, And not impute this yielding to light love, Which the dark night hath so discovered.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: Lady, by yonder blessed moon I swear, That tips with silver all these fruit-tree tops-
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: JULIET
utterances.text: O, swear not by the moon, th' inconstant moon, That monthly changes in her circled orb, Lest that thy love prove likewise variable.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: What shall I swear by?
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: JULIET
utterances.text: Do not swear at all; Or if thou wilt, swear by thy gracious self, Which is the god of my idolatry, And I'll believe thee.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: If my heart's dear love-
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: JULIET
utterances.text: Well, do not swear. Although I joy in thee, I have no joy of this contract to-night. It is too rash, too unadvis'd, too sudden; Too like the lightning, which doth cease to be Ere one can say 'It lightens.' Sweet, good night! This bud of love, by summer's ripening breath, May prove a beauteous flow'r when next we meet. Good night, good night! As sweet repose and rest Come to thy heart as that within my breast!
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: O, wilt thou leave me so unsatisfied?
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: JULIET
utterances.text: What satisfaction canst thou have to-night?
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: Th' exchange of thy love's faithful vow for mine.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: JULIET
utterances.text: I gave thee mine before thou didst request it; And yet I would it were to give again.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: Would'st thou withdraw it? For what purpose, love?
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: JULIET
utterances.text: But to be frank and give it thee again. And yet I wish but for the thing I have. My bounty is as boundless as the sea, My love as deep; the more I give to thee, The more I have, for both are infinite. I hear some noise within. Dear love, adieu! [Nurse] calls within. Anon, good nurse! Sweet Montague, be true. Stay but a little, I will come again.                [Exit.]
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: O blessed, blessed night! I am afeard, Being in night, all this is but a dream, Too flattering-sweet to be substantial.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: Enter Juliet above.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: JULIET
utterances.text: Three words, dear Romeo, and good night indeed. If that thy bent of love be honourable, Thy purpose marriage, send me word to-morrow, By one that I'll procure to come to thee, Where and what time thou wilt perform the rite; And all my fortunes at thy foot I'll lay And follow thee my lord throughout the world.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: NURSE
utterances.text: (within) Madam!
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: JULIET
utterances.text: I come, anon.- But if thou meanest not well, I do beseech thee-
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: NURSE
utterances.text: (within) Madam!
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: JULIET
utterances.text: By-and-by I come.- To cease thy suit and leave me to my grief. To-morrow will I send.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: So thrive my soul-
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: JULIET
utterances.text: A thousand times good night!                        Exit.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: A thousand times the worse, to want thy light! Love goes toward love as schoolboys from their books; But love from love, towards school with heavy looks.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: Enter Juliet again, [above].
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: JULIET
utterances.text: Hist! Romeo, hist! O for a falconer's voice To lure this tassel-gentle back again! Bondage is hoarse and may not speak aloud; Else would I tear the cave where Echo lies, And make her airy tongue more hoarse than mine With repetition of my Romeo's name. Romeo!
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: It is my soul that calls upon my name. How silver-sweet sound lovers' tongues by night, Like softest music to attending ears!
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: JULIET
utterances.text: Romeo!
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: My dear?
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: JULIET
utterances.text: At what o'clock to-morrow Shall I send to thee?
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: By the hour of nine.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: JULIET
utterances.text: I will not fail. 'Tis twenty years till then. I have forgot why I did call thee back.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: Let me stand here till thou remember it.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: JULIET
utterances.text: I shall forget, to have thee still stand there, Rememb'ring how I love thy company.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: And I'll still stay, to have thee still forget, Forgetting any other home but this.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: JULIET
utterances.text: 'Tis almost morning. I would have thee gone- And yet no farther than a wanton's bird, That lets it hop a little from her hand, Like a poor prisoner in his twisted gyves, And with a silk thread plucks it back again, So loving-jealous of his liberty.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: I would I were thy bird.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: JULIET
utterances.text: Sweet, so would I. Yet I should kill thee with much cherishing. Good night, good night! Parting is such sweet sorrow, That I shall say good night till it be morrow.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: Exit.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: Sleep dwell upon thine eyes, peace in thy breast! Would I were sleep and peace, so sweet to rest! Hence will I to my ghostly father's cell, His help to crave and my dear hap to tell.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: Exit
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

scenes.text:
[Enter Romeo.]
ROMEO. He jests at scars that never felt a wound.
[Enter Juliet above at a window.]
[But soft! What light through yonder window breaks?]
[It is the East, and Juliet is the sun!]
[Arise, fair sun, and kill the envious moon,]
[Who is already sick and pale with grief]
[That thou her maid art far more fair than she.]
[Be not her maid, since she is envious.]
[Her vestal livery is but sick and green,]
[And none but fools do wear it. Cast it off.]
[It is my lady; O, it is my love!]
[O that she knew she were!]
[She speaks, yet she says nothing. What of that?]
[Her eye discourses; I will answer it.]
[I am too bold; 'tis not to me she speaks.]
[Two of the fairest stars in all the heaven,]
[Having some business, do entreat her eyes]
[To twinkle in their spheres till they return.]
[What if her eyes were there, they in her head?]
[The brightness of her cheek would shame those stars]
[As daylight doth a lamp; her eyes in heaven]
[Would through the airy region stream so bright]
[That birds would sing and think it were not night.]
[See how she leans her cheek upon her hand!]
[O that I were a glove upon that hand,]
[That I might touch that cheek!]
JULIET. Ah me!
ROMEO. She speaks. O, speak again, bright angel! for thou art As glorious to this night, being o'er my head, As is a winged messenger of heaven Unto the white-upturned wond'ring eyes Of mortals that fall back to gaze on him When he bestrides the lazy-pacing clouds And sails upon the bosom of the air.
JULIET. O Romeo, Romeo! wherefore art thou Romeo? Deny thy father and refuse thy name! Or, if thou wilt not, be but sworn my love, And I'll no longer be a Capulet.
ROMEO. [aside] Shall I hear more, or shall I speak at this?
JULIET. 'Tis but thy name that is my enemy. Thou art thyself, though not a Montague. What's Montague? it is nor hand, nor foot, Nor arm, nor face, nor any other part Belonging to a man. O, be some other name! What's in a name? That which we call a rose By any other name would smell as sweet. So Romeo would, were he not Romeo call'd, Retain that dear perfection which he owes Without that title. Romeo, doff thy name; And for that name, which is no part of thee, Take all myself.
ROMEO. I take thee at thy word. Call me but love, and I'll be new baptiz'd; Henceforth I never will be Romeo.
JULIET. What man art thou that, thus bescreen'd in night, So stumblest on my counsel?
ROMEO. By a name I know not how to tell thee who I am. My name, dear saint, is hateful to myself, Because it is an enemy to thee. Had I it written, I would tear the word.
JULIET. My ears have yet not drunk a hundred words Of that tongue's utterance, yet I know the sound. Art thou not Romeo, and a Montague?
ROMEO. Neither, fair saint, if either thee dislike.
JULIET. How cam'st thou hither, tell me, and wherefore? The orchard walls are high and hard to climb, And the place death, considering who thou art, If any of my kinsmen find thee here.
ROMEO. With love's light wings did I o'erperch these walls; For stony limits cannot hold love out, And what love can do, that dares love attempt. Therefore thy kinsmen are no let to me.
JULIET. If they do see thee, they will murther thee.
ROMEO. Alack, there lies more peril in thine eye Than twenty of their swords! Look thou but sweet, And I am proof against their enmity.
JULIET. I would not for the world they saw thee here.
ROMEO. I have night's cloak to hide me from their sight; And but thou love me, let them find me here. My life were better ended by their hate Than death prorogued, wanting of thy love.
JULIET. By whose direction found'st thou out this place?
ROMEO. By love, that first did prompt me to enquire. He lent me counsel, and I lent him eyes. I am no pilot; yet, wert thou as far As that vast shore wash'd with the farthest sea, I would adventure for such merchandise.
JULIET. Thou knowest the mask of night is on my face; Else would a maiden blush bepaint my cheek For that which thou hast heard me speak to-night. Fain would I dwell on form- fain, fain deny What I have spoke; but farewell compliment! Dost thou love me, I know thou wilt say 'Ay'; And I will take thy word. Yet, if thou swear'st, Thou mayst prove false. At lovers' perjuries, They say Jove laughs. O gentle Romeo, If thou dost love, pronounce it faithfully. Or if thou thinkest I am too quickly won, I'll frown, and be perverse, and say thee nay, So thou wilt woo; but else, not for the world. In truth, fair Montague, I am too fond, And therefore thou mayst think my haviour light; But trust me, gentleman, I'll prove more true Than those that have more cunning to be strange. I should have been more strange, I must confess, But that thou overheard'st, ere I was ware, My true-love passion. Therefore pardon me, And not impute this yielding to light love, Which the dark night hath so discovered.
ROMEO. Lady, by yonder blessed moon I swear, That tips with silver all these fruit-tree tops-
JULIET. O, swear not by the moon, th' inconstant moon, That monthly changes in her circled orb, Lest that thy love prove likewise variable.
ROMEO. What shall I swear by?
JULIET. Do not swear at all; Or if thou wilt, swear by thy gracious self, Which is the god of my idolatry, And I'll believe thee.
ROMEO. If my heart's dear love-
JULIET. Well, do not swear. Although I joy in thee, I have no joy of this contract to-night. It is too rash, too unadvis'd, too sudden; Too like the lightning, which doth cease to be Ere one can say 'It lightens.' Sweet, good night! This bud of love, by summer's ripening breath, May prove a beauteous flow'r when next we meet. Good night, good night! As sweet repose and rest Come to thy heart as that within my breast!
ROMEO. O, wilt thou leave me so unsatisfied?
JULIET. What satisfaction canst thou have to-night?
ROMEO. Th' exchange of thy love's faithful vow for mine.
JULIET. I gave thee mine before thou didst request it; And yet I would it were to give again.
ROMEO. Would'st thou withdraw it? For what purpose, love?
JULIET. But to be frank and give it thee again. And yet I wish but for the thing I have. My bounty is as boundless as the sea, My love as deep; the more I give to thee, The more I have, for both are infinite. I hear some noise within. Dear love, adieu! [Nurse] calls within. Anon, good nurse! Sweet Montague, be true. Stay but a little, I will come again.                [Exit.]
ROMEO. O blessed, blessed night! I am afeard, Being in night, all this is but a dream, Too flattering-sweet to be substantial.
[Enter Juliet above.]
JULIET. Three words, dear Romeo, and good night indeed. If that thy bent of love be honourable, Thy purpose marriage, send me word to-morrow, By one that I'll procure to come to thee, Where and what time thou wilt perform the rite; And all my fortunes at thy foot I'll lay And follow thee my lord throughout the world.
NURSE. (within) Madam!
JULIET. I come, anon.- But if thou meanest not well, I do beseech thee-
NURSE. (within) Madam!
JULIET. By-and-by I come.- To cease thy suit and leave me to my grief. To-morrow will I send.
ROMEO. So thrive my soul-
JULIET. A thousand times good night!                        Exit.
ROMEO. A thousand times the worse, to want thy light! Love goes toward love as schoolboys from their books; But love from love, towards school with heavy looks.
[Enter Juliet again, [above].]
JULIET. Hist! Romeo, hist! O for a falconer's voice To lure this tassel-gentle back again! Bondage is hoarse and may not speak aloud; Else would I tear the cave where Echo lies, And make her airy tongue more hoarse than mine With repetition of my Romeo's name. Romeo!
ROMEO. It is my soul that calls upon my name. How silver-sweet sound lovers' tongues by night, Like softest music to attending ears!
JULIET. Romeo!
ROMEO. My dear?
JULIET. At what o'clock to-morrow Shall I send to thee?
ROMEO. By the hour of nine.
JULIET. I will not fail. 'Tis twenty years till then. I have forgot why I did call thee back.
ROMEO. Let me stand here till thou remember it.
JULIET. I shall forget, to have thee still stand there, Rememb'ring how I love thy company.
ROMEO. And I'll still stay, to have thee still forget, Forgetting any other home but this.
JULIET. 'Tis almost morning. I would have thee gone- And yet no farther than a wanton's bird, That lets it hop a little from her hand, Like a poor prisoner in his twisted gyves, And with a silk thread plucks it back again, So loving-jealous of his liberty.
ROMEO. I would I were thy bird.
JULIET. Sweet, so would I. Yet I should kill thee with much cherishing. Good night, good night! Parting is such sweet sorrow, That I shall say good night till it be morrow.
[Exit.]
ROMEO. Sleep dwell upon thine eyes, peace in thy breast! Would I were sleep and peace, so sweet to rest! Hence will I to my ghostly father's cell, His help to crave and my dear hap to tell.
[Exit]
scenes.scene_summary: Romeo and Juliet confess love and plan marriage in the balcony scene.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: Enter Friar, [Laurence] alone, with a basket.
utterances.scene_summary: Friar Laurence agrees to marry the lovers, hoping to end the feud.

utterances.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
utterances.text: The grey-ey'd morn smiles on the frowning night, Check'ring the Eastern clouds with streaks of light; And flecked darkness like a drunkard reels From forth day's path and Titan's fiery wheels. Non, ere the sun advance his burning eye The day to cheer and night's dank dew to dry, I must up-fill this osier cage of ours With baleful weeds and precious-juiced flowers. The earth that's nature's mother is her tomb. What is her burying gave, that is her womb; And from her womb children of divers kind We sucking on her natural bosom find; Many for many virtues excellent, None but for some, and yet all different. O, mickle is the powerful grace that lies In plants, herbs, stones, and their true qualities; For naught so vile that on the earth doth live But to the earth some special good doth give; Nor aught so good but, strain'd from that fair use, Revolts from true birth, stumbling on abuse. Virtue itself turns vice, being misapplied, And vice sometime's by action dignified. Within the infant rind of this small flower Poison hath residence, and medicine power; For this, being smelt, with that part cheers each part; Being tasted, slays all senses with the heart. Two such opposed kings encamp them still In man as well as herbs- grace and rude will; And where the worser is predominant, Full soon the canker death eats up that plant.
utterances.scene_summary: Friar Laurence agrees to marry the lovers, hoping to end the feud.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: Enter Romeo.
utterances.scene_summary: Friar Laurence agrees to marry the lovers, hoping to end the feud.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: Good morrow, father.
utterances.scene_summary: Friar Laurence agrees to marry the lovers, hoping to end the feud.

utterances.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
utterances.text: Benedicite! What early tongue so sweet saluteth me? Young son, it argues a distempered head So soon to bid good morrow to thy bed. Care keeps his watch in every old man's eye, And where care lodges sleep will never lie; But where unbruised youth with unstuff'd brain Doth couch his limbs, there golden sleep doth reign. Therefore thy earliness doth me assure Thou art uprous'd with some distemp'rature; Or if not so, then here I hit it right- Our Romeo hath not been in bed to-night.
utterances.scene_summary: Friar Laurence agrees to marry the lovers, hoping to end the feud.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: That last is true-the sweeter rest was mine.
utterances.scene_summary: Friar Laurence agrees to marry the lovers, hoping to end the feud.

utterances.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
utterances.text: God pardon sin! Wast thou with Rosaline?
utterances.scene_summary: Friar Laurence agrees to marry the lovers, hoping to end the feud.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: With Rosaline, my ghostly father? No. I have forgot that name, and that name's woe.
utterances.scene_summary: Friar Laurence agrees to marry the lovers, hoping to end the feud.

utterances.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
utterances.text: That's my good son! But where hast thou been then?
utterances.scene_summary: Friar Laurence agrees to marry the lovers, hoping to end the feud.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: I'll tell thee ere thou ask it me again. I have been feasting with mine enemy, Where on a sudden one hath wounded me That's by me wounded. Both our remedies Within thy help and holy physic lies. I bear no hatred, blessed man, for, lo, My intercession likewise steads my foe.
utterances.scene_summary: Friar Laurence agrees to marry the lovers, hoping to end the feud.

utterances.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
utterances.text: Be plain, good son, and homely in thy drift Riddling confession finds but riddling shrift.
utterances.scene_summary: Friar Laurence agrees to marry the lovers, hoping to end the feud.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: Then plainly know my heart's dear love is set On the fair daughter of rich Capulet; As mine on hers, so hers is set on mine, And all combin'd, save what thou must combine By holy marriage. When, and where, and how We met, we woo'd, and made exchange of vow, I'll tell thee as we pass; but this I pray, That thou consent to marry us to-day.
utterances.scene_summary: Friar Laurence agrees to marry the lovers, hoping to end the feud.

utterances.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
utterances.text: Holy Saint Francis! What a change is here! Is Rosaline, that thou didst love so dear, So soon forsaken? Young men's love then lies Not truly in their hearts, but in their eyes. Jesu Maria! What a deal of brine Hath wash'd thy sallow cheeks for Rosaline! How much salt water thrown away in waste, To season love, that of it doth not taste! The sun not yet thy sighs from heaven clears, Thy old groans ring yet in mine ancient ears. Lo, here upon thy cheek the stain doth sit Of an old tear that is not wash'd off yet. If e'er thou wast thyself, and these woes thine, Thou and these woes were all for Rosaline. And art thou chang'd? Pronounce this sentence then: Women may fall when there's no strength in men.
utterances.scene_summary: Friar Laurence agrees to marry the lovers, hoping to end the feud.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: Thou chid'st me oft for loving Rosaline.
utterances.scene_summary: Friar Laurence agrees to marry the lovers, hoping to end the feud.

utterances.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
utterances.text: For doting, not for loving, pupil mine.
utterances.scene_summary: Friar Laurence agrees to marry the lovers, hoping to end the feud.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: And bad'st me bury love.
utterances.scene_summary: Friar Laurence agrees to marry the lovers, hoping to end the feud.

utterances.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
utterances.text: Not in a grave To lay one in, another out to have.
utterances.scene_summary: Friar Laurence agrees to marry the lovers, hoping to end the feud.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: I pray thee chide not. She whom I love now Doth grace for grace and love for love allow. The other did not so.
utterances.scene_summary: Friar Laurence agrees to marry the lovers, hoping to end the feud.

utterances.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
utterances.text: O, she knew well Thy love did read by rote, that could not spell. But come, young waverer, come go with me. In one respect I'll thy assistant be; For this alliance may so happy prove To turn your households' rancour to pure love.
utterances.scene_summary: Friar Laurence agrees to marry the lovers, hoping to end the feud.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: O, let us hence! I stand on sudden haste.
utterances.scene_summary: Friar Laurence agrees to marry the lovers, hoping to end the feud.

utterances.speaker: FRIAR LAURENCE
utterances.text: Wisely, and slow. They stumble that run fast.
utterances.scene_summary: Friar Laurence agrees to marry the lovers, hoping to end the feud.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: Exeunt.
utterances.scene_summary: Friar Laurence agrees to marry the lovers, hoping to end the feud.

scenes.text:
[Enter Friar, [Laurence] alone, with a basket.]
FRIAR LAURENCE. The grey-ey'd morn smiles on the frowning night, Check'ring the Eastern clouds with streaks of light; And flecked darkness like a drunkard reels From forth day's path and Titan's fiery wheels. Non, ere the sun advance his burning eye The day to cheer and night's dank dew to dry, I must up-fill this osier cage of ours With baleful weeds and precious-juiced flowers. The earth that's nature's mother is her tomb. What is her burying gave, that is her womb; And from her womb children of divers kind We sucking on her natural bosom find; Many for many virtues excellent, None but for some, and yet all different. O, mickle is the powerful grace that lies In plants, herbs, stones, and their true qualities; For naught so vile that on the earth doth live But to the earth some special good doth give; Nor aught so good but, strain'd from that fair use, Revolts from true birth, stumbling on abuse. Virtue itself turns vice, being misapplied, And vice sometime's by action dignified. Within the infant rind of this small flower Poison hath residence, and medicine power; For this, being smelt, with that part cheers each part; Being tasted, slays all senses with the heart. Two such opposed kings encamp them still In man as well as herbs- grace and rude will; And where the worser is predominant, Full soon the canker death eats up that plant.
[Enter Romeo.]
ROMEO. Good morrow, father.
FRIAR LAURENCE. Benedicite! What early tongue so sweet saluteth me? Young son, it argues a distempered head So soon to bid good morrow to thy bed. Care keeps his watch in every old man's eye, And where care lodges sleep will never lie; But where unbruised youth with unstuff'd brain Doth couch his limbs, there golden sleep doth reign. Therefore thy earliness doth me assure Thou art uprous'd with some distemp'rature; Or if not so, then here I hit it right- Our Romeo hath not been in bed to-night.
ROMEO. That last is true-the sweeter rest was mine.
FRIAR LAURENCE. God pardon sin! Wast thou with Rosaline?
ROMEO. With Rosaline, my ghostly father? No. I have forgot that name, and that name's woe.
FRIAR LAURENCE. That's my good son! But where hast thou been then?
ROMEO. I'll tell thee ere thou ask it me again. I have been feasting with mine enemy, Where on a sudden one hath wounded me That's by me wounded. Both our remedies Within thy help and holy physic lies. I bear no hatred, blessed man, for, lo, My intercession likewise steads my foe.
FRIAR LAURENCE. Be plain, good son, and homely in thy drift Riddling confession finds but riddling shrift.
ROMEO. Then plainly know my heart's dear love is set On the fair daughter of rich Capulet; As mine on hers, so hers is set on mine, And all combin'd, save what thou must combine By holy marriage. When, and where, and how We met, we woo'd, and made exchange of vow, I'll tell thee as we pass; but this I pray, That thou consent to marry us to-day.
FRIAR LAURENCE. Holy Saint Francis! What a change is here! Is Rosaline, that thou didst love so dear, So soon forsaken? Young men's love then lies Not truly in their hearts, but in their eyes. Jesu Maria! What a deal of brine Hath wash'd thy sallow cheeks for Rosaline! How much salt water thrown away in waste, To season love, that of it doth not taste! The sun not yet thy sighs from heaven clears, Thy old groans ring yet in mine ancient ears. Lo, here upon thy cheek the stain doth sit Of an old tear that is not wash'd off yet. If e'er thou wast thyself, and these woes thine, Thou and these woes were all for Rosaline. And art thou chang'd? Pronounce this sentence then: Women may fall when there's no strength in men.
ROMEO. Thou chid'st me oft for loving Rosaline.
FRIAR LAURENCE. For doting, not for loving, pupil mine.
ROMEO. And bad'st me bury love.
FRIAR LAURENCE. Not in a grave To lay one in, another out to have.
ROMEO. I pray thee chide not. She whom I love now Doth grace for grace and love for love allow. The other did not so.
FRIAR LAURENCE. O, she knew well Thy love did read by rote, that could not spell. But come, young waverer, come go with me. In one respect I'll thy assistant be; For this alliance may so happy prove To turn your households' rancour to pure love.
ROMEO. O, let us hence! I stand on sudden haste.
FRIAR LAURENCE. Wisely, and slow. They stumble that run fast.
[Exeunt.]
scenes.scene_summary: Friar Laurence agrees to marry the lovers, hoping to end the feud.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: Enter Benvolio and Mercutio.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: MERCUTIO
utterances.text: Where the devil should this Romeo be? Came he not home to-night?
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: BENVOLIO
utterances.text: Not to his father's. I spoke with his man.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: MERCUTIO
utterances.text: Why, that same pale hard-hearted wench, that Rosaline, Torments him so that he will sure run mad.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: BENVOLIO
utterances.text: Tybalt, the kinsman to old Capulet, Hath sent a letter to his father's house.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: MERCUTIO
utterances.text: A challenge, on my life.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: BENVOLIO
utterances.text: Romeo will answer it.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: MERCUTIO
utterances.text: Any man that can write may answer a letter.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: BENVOLIO
utterances.text: Nay, he will answer the letter's master, how he dares, being dared.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: MERCUTIO
utterances.text: Alas, poor Romeo, he is already dead! stabb'd with a white wench's black eye; shot through the ear with a love song; the very pin of his heart cleft with the blind bow-boy's butt-shaft; and is he a man to encounter Tybalt?
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: BENVOLIO
utterances.text: Why, what is Tybalt?
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: MERCUTIO
utterances.text: More than Prince of Cats, I can tell you. O, he's the courageous captain of compliments. He fights as you sing pricksong-keeps time, distance, and proportion; rests me his minim rest, one, two, and the third in your bosom! the very butcher of a silk button, a duellist, a duellist! a gentleman of the very first house, of the first and second cause. Ah, the immortal passado! the punto reverse! the hay.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: BENVOLIO
utterances.text: The what?
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: MERCUTIO
utterances.text: The pox of such antic, lisping, affecting fantasticoes- these new tuners of accent! 'By Jesu, a very good blade! a very tall man! a very good whore!' Why, is not this a lamentable thing, grandsir, that we should be thus afflicted with these strange flies, these fashion-mongers, these pardona-mi's, who stand so much on the new form that they cannot sit at ease on the old bench? O, their bones, their bones!
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: STAGE_DIRECTION
utterances.text: Enter Romeo.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: BENVOLIO
utterances.text: Here comes Romeo! here comes Romeo!
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: MERCUTIO
utterances.text: Without his roe, like a dried herring. O flesh, flesh, how art thou fishified! Now is he for the numbers that Petrarch flowed in. Laura, to his lady, was but a kitchen wench (marry, she had a better love to berhyme her), Dido a dowdy, Cleopatra a gypsy, Helen and Hero hildings and harlots, This be a gray eye or so, but not to the purpose. Signior Romeo, bon jour! There's a French salutation to your French slop. You gave us the counterfeit fairly last night.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: Good morrow to you both. What counterfeit did I give you?
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: MERCUTIO
utterances.text: The slip, sir, the slip. Can you not conceive?
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: Pardon, good Mercutio. My business was great, and in such a case as mine a man may strain courtesy.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: MERCUTIO
utterances.text: That's as much as to say, such a case as yours constrains a man to bow in the hams.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: Meaning, to cursy.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: MERCUTIO
utterances.text: Thou hast most kindly hit it.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: A most courteous exposition.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: MERCUTIO
utterances.text: Nay, I am the very pink of courtesy.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: Pink for flower.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: MERCUTIO
utterances.text: Right.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: Why, then is my pump well-flower'd.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: MERCUTIO
utterances.text: Well said! Follow me this jest now till thou hast worn out thy pump, that, when the single sole of it is worn, the jest may remain, after the wearing, solely singular.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: O single-sold jest, solely singular for the singleness!
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: MERCUTIO
utterances.text: Come between us, good Benvolio! My wits faint.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: Swits and spurs, swits and spurs! or I'll cry a match.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: MERCUTIO
utterances.text: Nay, if our wits run the wild-goose chase, I am done; for thou hast more of the wild goose in one of thy wits than, I am sure, I have in my whole five. Was I with you there for the goose?
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: Thou wast never with me for anything when thou wast not there for the goose.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: MERCUTIO
utterances.text: I will bite thee by the ear for that jest.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: Nay, good goose, bite not!
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: MERCUTIO
utterances.text: Thy wit is a very bitter sweeting; it is a most sharp sauce.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: And is it not, then, well serv'd in to a sweet goose?
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: MERCUTIO
utterances.text: O, here's a wit of cheveril, that stretches from an inch narrow to an ell broad!
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: I stretch it out for that word 'broad,' which, added to the goose, proves thee far and wide a broad goose.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: MERCUTIO
utterances.text: Why, is not this better now than groaning for love? Now art thou sociable, now art thou Romeo; now art thou what thou art, by art as well as by nature. For this drivelling love is like a great natural that runs lolling up and down to hide his bauble in a hole.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: BENVOLIO
utterances.text: Stop there, stop there!
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: MERCUTIO
utterances.text: Thou desirest me to stop in my tale against the hair.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: BENVOLIO
utterances.text: Thou wouldst else have made thy tale large.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: MERCUTIO
utterances.text: O, thou art deceiv'd! I would have made it short; for I was come to the whole depth of my tale, and meant indeed to occupy the argument no longer.

utterances.speaker: NURSE

utterances.speaker: MERCUTIO

utterances.speaker: MERCUTIO

utterances.speaker: NURSE

utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: One, gentlewoman, that God hath made for himself to mar.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: NURSE

utterances.speaker: ROMEO

utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: NURSE
utterances.text: You say well.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: MERCUTIO

utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: NURSE
utterances.text: If you be he, sir, I desire some confidence with you.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: BENVOLIO
utterances.text: She will endite him to some supper.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: MERCUTIO
utterances.text: A bawd, a bawd, a bawd! So ho!
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: What hast thou found?
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: MERCUTIO
utterances.text: No hare, sir; unless a hare, sir, in a lenten pie, that is something stale and hoar ere it be spent He walks by them and sings. An old hare hoar, And an old hare hoar, Is very good meat in Lent; But a hare that is hoar Is too much for a score When it hoars ere it be spent. Romeo, will you come to your father's? We'll to dinner thither.

utterances.speaker: MERCUTIO

utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: NURSE
utterances.text: Marry, farewell! I Pray you, Sir, what saucy merchant was this that was so full of his ropery?
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: A gentleman, nurse, that loves to hear himself talk and will speak more in a minute than he will stand to in a month.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: NURSE
utterances.text: An 'a speak anything against me, I'll take him down, an 'a were lustier than he is, and twenty such jacks; and if I cannot, I'll find those that shall. Scurvy knave! I am none of his flirt-gills; I am none of his skains-mates. And thou must stand by too, and suffer every knave to use me at his pleasure!
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: PETER
utterances.text: I saw no man use you at his pleasure. If I had, my weapon should quickly have been out, I warrant you. I dare draw as soon as another man, if I see occasion in a good quarrel, and the law on my side.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: NURSE
utterances.text: Now, afore God, I am so vexed that every part about me quivers. Scurvy knave! Pray you, sir, a word; and, as I told you, my young lady bid me enquire you out. What she bid me say, I will keep to myself; but first let me tell ye, if ye should lead her into a fool's paradise, as they say, it were a very gross kind of behaviour, as they say; for the gentlewoman is young; and therefore, if you should deal double with her, truly it were an ill thing to be off'red to any gentlewoman, and very weak dealing.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: Nurse, commend me to thy lady and mistress. I protest unto thee-
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: NURSE
utterances.text: Good heart, and i' faith I will tell her as much. Lord, Lord! she will be a joyful woman.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: What wilt thou tell her, nurse? Thou dost not mark me.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: NURSE
utterances.text: I will tell her, sir, that you do protest, which, as I take it, is a gentlemanlike offer.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: Bid her devise Some means to come to shrift this afternoon; And there she shall at Friar Laurence' cell Be shriv'd and married. Here is for thy pains.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: NURSE
utterances.text: No, truly, sir; not a penny.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: Go to! I say you shall.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: NURSE
utterances.text: This afternoon, sir? Well, she shall be there.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: And stay, good nurse, behind the abbey wall. Within this hour my man shall be with thee And bring thee cords made like a tackled stair, Which to the high topgallant of my joy Must be my convoy in the secret night.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: FAREWELL
utterances.text: Be trusty, and I'll quit thy pains.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: FAREWELL
utterances.text: Commend me to thy mistress.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: NURSE
utterances.text: Now God in heaven bless thee! Hark you, sir.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: What say'st thou, my dear nurse?
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: NURSE
utterances.text: Is your man secret? Did you ne'er hear say, Two may keep counsel, putting one away?
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: I warrant thee my man's as true as steel.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: NURSE
utterances.text: Well, sir, my mistress is the sweetest lady. Lord, Lord! when 'twas a little prating thing- O, there is a nobleman in town, one Paris, that would fain lay knife aboard; but she, good soul, had as lieve see a toad, a very toad, as see him. I anger her sometimes, and tell her that Paris is the properer man; but I'll warrant you, when I say so, she looks as pale as any clout in the versal world. Doth not rosemary and Romeo begin both with a letter?

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: Ay, nurse; what of that? Both with an R.

utterances.speaker: NURSE
utterances.text: Ah, mocker! that's the dog's name. R is for the- No; I know it begins with some other letter; and she hath the prettiest sententious of it, of you and rosemary, that it would do you good to hear it.
utterances.scene_summary: Romeo arranges marriage plans through the Nurse.

utterances.speaker: ROMEO
utterances.text: Commend me to thy lady.
